flashcards.back: Inheritance is an OOP mechanism that allows a derived class to inherit properties and behaviors (data members and member functions) from an existing base class, promoting code reuse and establishing IS-A relationship.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the 'protected' access specifier?
flashcards.back: Protected members are accessible within the class and its derived classes but not from outside. It provides a middle ground between private (most restrictive) and public (least restrictive) access.

flashcards.front: What is a base class in C++?
flashcards.back: A base class (also called parent class or superclass) is the existing class from which other classes inherit. It defines common attributes and behaviors that can be reused by derived classes.

flashcards.front: What is a derived class in C++?
flashcards.back: A derived class (also called child class or subclass) is a new class that inherits properties and behaviors from an existing base class. It can add new members or modify inherited ones.
Single inheritance occurs when a derived class inherits from only one base class. Syntax: class Derived : public Base { };
Multiple inheritance occurs when a derived class inherits from more than one base class. Syntax: class Derived : public Base1, public Base2 { };
Multilevel inheritance involves a chain of inheritance: Class A → Class B → Class C, where C inherits from B, and B inherits from A.
Hierarchical inheritance occurs when multiple derived classes inherit from a single base class, forming a tree-like structure with one parent and multiple children.
Hybrid inheritance is a combination of two or more types of inheritance (e.g., combining multiple and multilevel inheritance). It often requires virtual base classes to avoid ambiguity.

flashcards.front: What are visibility modes in inheritance?
flashcards.back: Visibility modes (public, protected, private) determine how base class members are accessed in the derived class. Public: remains public/protected/private; Protected: becomes protected/private; Private: becomes private in derived class.

flashcards.front: What is the order of constructor execution in derived classes?
flashcards.back: Constructors are executed in order of derivation: base class constructor executes first, then member object constructors (if any), and finally the derived class constructor executes.

flashcards.front: What is the order of destructor execution in derived classes?
flashcards.back: Destructors execute in reverse order of constructors: derived class destructor runs first, then member object destructors, and finally base class destructor.

flashcards.front: What is a virtual base class?
flashcards.back: A virtual base class is used in multiple inheritance to ensure that only one copy of the base class's members exists in the derived class, preventing ambiguity and data redundancy.

flashcards.front: How do you resolve ambiguity in multiple inheritance?
flashcards.back: Ambiguity occurs when derived class inherits same members from multiple base classes. It is resolved using scope resolution operator (::) to specify which base class's member to use, e.g., obj.Base1::memberName.

Runtime polymorphism is achieved through inheritance and virtual functions, allowing the program to determine which function to call at runtime based on the actual object type, not the reference type.
